content.text: Why Claude Integration Work Benefits from Pairing

Most of what is wrong with a Claude integration is not in the code — it is in the prompt, the eval design, or the gap between the behaviour spec and what was actually implemented. These errors are invisible to the person who wrote them, because the writer's mental model fills in the gaps. A second person with a different mental model sees the gaps immediately.

Pairing on Claude integration is not about catching syntax errors. It is about catching the prompt constraint that was clear in the writer's head but ambiguous on the page, and the eval case that tests the happy path but misses the failure mode.

Claude integration also has a feedback loop that benefits from two people: write a prompt change, run the eval, read the results, decide what to change next. A driver who is also reading results, updating the spec, and writing the next change is cognitively overloaded. An observer who focuses exclusively on the results and the spec catches things the driver misses.
The Driver–Observer Model for Claude Work

attrs.alt: Pairing roles: Driver writes code and prompt, runs evals, commits hashes. Observer reads behaviour spec, checks eval results, flags drift. Switch every 25 minutes.

content.text: The driver–observer model for Claude integration differs from standard coding pairs in one key respect: the observer's primary reference is the behaviour spec, not the code. The observer's job is to check whether what the driver is building actually implements what the spec says, using the eval results as evidence.
The Pairing Session Structure
Opening (5 minutes)
Both engineers read the behaviour spec together — not the code, not the prompt
Identify which must-do items are not yet covered by the current eval suite
Set the session goal: 'By the end of this session, MUST-003 and MUST-005 should pass'
The driver opens the current prompt and the eval runner
Working Cycle (25-minute pomodoros)
Each pomodoro follows a strict prompt-eval-spec loop:
Driver:
make one targeted change to the prompt or integration code. Speak aloud what you are changing and why.
Driver:
run the eval suite immediately.
Observer:
read the eval output. Check every failure case against the must-not-do list. If any failure is a must-not-do violation, say so before the driver reads the results.
Both:
discuss whether the result meets the spec. Decide whether to commit this change or iterate.
Driver:
if committing — update the prompt_registry.json hash, commit, then continue.
Switching

Switch driver–observer roles at every pomodoro boundary (25 minutes). Do not let the person with the deepest context stay as driver indefinitely — the whole point is that the observer brings fresh attention.
Closing (10 minutes)
Run the full eval suite one final time
Observer reads every must-do item: is it tested? Is it passing?
Note any must-not-do items still untested — these become the opening goal for the next session
Commit the session's work including updated prompt_registry.json
What the Observer Watches For
The observer's attention is distributed across four concerns that the driver cannot simultaneously track:
Concern
Observer checks
Signal to intervene
Spec drift
Does the prompt match the must-do list?
Driver adds a constraint not in the spec
Eval coverage
Are all must-do items covered by test cases?
Driver marks a must-do as done without an eval case
Failure patterns
Do failures cluster around a specific input type?
3+ failures with the same root cause
Must-not-do violations
Does any failure output a must-not-do behaviour?
Any failure that is a must-not-do, regardless of rate
Prompt-Eval Pairing Workflow in Code
The following workflow captures the cycle the driver runs in code, with the observer watching the eval output at each step.
When to Pause and Revisit the Spec
There are four signals that should cause the pair to stop coding and re-read the behaviour spec together:
The eval suite passes but the behaviour feels wrong
: the eval suite is not testing the right thing. The spec needs to be checked for gaps.
Three consecutive prompt iterations do not improve the failing case
: the root cause is probably in the spec, not the prompt. The must-do item may be ambiguous.
A must-not-do violation appears in eval output
: stop immediately. Do not continue until this is understood. A must-not-do violation in eval is a production safety event in waiting.
The observer and driver disagree about whether a failure is acceptable
: this is a spec ambiguity. Write the resolution into the spec before continuing.
Pairing for Prompt Debugging

Prompt debugging — finding why the model produces an unexpected output for a specific input — is more effective with two people because the search space is large and the two people will generate different hypotheses.
Common Pairing Anti-Patterns for Claude Work
Prompting instead of pairing
: using Claude (the model) to write prompts during the pairing session without reading the output against the spec is not pairing — it is prompt golf with an audience.
Eval-driven development without spec anchoring
: making the eval suite pass without checking that the eval suite actually tests what the spec requires produces a feature that passes tests but fails in production.
Observer as silent spectator
: if the observer has not spoken in 10 minutes, the session has degraded into solo work with an audience. The observer must engage actively.
Treating every failure as an emergency
: not every failing eval case requires an immediate fix. The pair should triage: is this a must-not-do violation (stop everything) or a must-do gap (add to backlog)?
Summary

Pairing for Claude integration work assigns a Driver (who writes code and prompt changes, runs evals, and commits hashes) and an Observer (who reads the behaviour spec, checks eval results against must-not-do items, and flags drift). Sessions follow a prompt-eval-spec loop with 25-minute role switches. The Observer's four key checks are spec drift, eval coverage gaps, failure case patterns, and must-not-do violations. Prompt debugging is externalised using a hypothesis generator so both engineers work from the same model of the root cause. The most common anti-pattern is treating eval pass rate as the primary success criterion without anchoring to what the spec actually requires.
